title: Evidence for Hierarchical Black Hole Mergers in the Second LIGO–Virgo Gravitational Wave Catalog
body: Abstract We study the population properties of merging binary black holes in the second LIGO–Virgo Gravitational-Wave Transient Catalog assuming they were all formed dynamically in gravitationally bound clusters. Using a phenomenological population model, we infer the mass and spin distribution of first-generation black holes, while self-consistently accounting for hierarchical mergers. Considering a range of cluster masses, we see compelling evidence for hierarchical mergers in clusters with escape velocities ≳100 km s −1 . For our most probable cluster mass, we find that the catalog contains at least one second-generation merger with 99% credibility. We find that the hierarchical model is preferred over an alternative model with no hierarchical mergers (Bayes factor <mml:math xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" overflow="scroll"> <mml:mi mathvariant="italic"></mml:mi> <mml:mo>></mml:mo> <mml:mn>1400</mml:mn> </mml:math> ) and that GW190521 is favored to contain two second-generation black holes with odds <mml:math xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" overflow="scroll"> <mml:mi mathvariant="italic"></mml:mi> <mml:mo>></mml:mo> <mml:mn>700</mml:mn> </mml:math> , and GW190519, GW190602, GW190620, and GW190706 are mixed-generation binaries with <mml:math xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" overflow="scroll"> <mml:mi mathvariant="italic"></mml:mi> <mml:mo>></mml:mo> <mml:mn>10</mml:mn> </mml:math> . However, our results depend strongly on the cluster escape velocity, with more modest evidence for hierarchical mergers when the escape velocity is ≲100 km s −1 . Assuming that all binary black holes are formed dynamically in globular clusters with escape velocities on the order of tens of km s −1 , GW190519 and GW190521 are favored to include a second-generation black hole with odds <mml:math xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" overflow="scroll"> <mml:mi mathvariant="italic"></mml:mi> <mml:mo>></mml:mo> <mml:mn>1</mml:mn> </mml:math> . In this case, we find that 99% of black holes from the inferred total population have masses that are less than 49 M ⊙ , and that this constraint is robust to our choice of prior on the maximum black hole mass. Physics Binary black hole Globular cluster Astrophysics Cluster (spacecraft) Black hole (networking) Population Binary number Gravitational wave Spin-flip Intermediate-mass black hole Range (aeronautics) Astronomy Star cluster Gravitation Mass distribution Stellar black hole Order (exchange) Hierarchical clustering
